note: Extracted from the Code For Good reference PDFs. Pillar descriptions are faithful condensations of each pillar’s intro; milestones and fast-track days are taken from the source.

pillars.title: Build AI-Augmented Skills
pillars.description: The most in-demand STEM graduates aren't those who know the most code — they're the ones who work with AI tools to produce 10x the output. Every employer now expects new hires to use AI productively from day one.
milestones: Install and use GitHub Copilot free tier (available to students via GitHub Education Pack)
Learn the SCALE Framework — apply it to every prompt you write from day
Learn the basics of LLM concepts: tokens, context windows, temperature, system prompts
Set up Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini — understand their strengths for different tasks
Study token economics: what tokens cost in money, latency, and carbon emissions
Use AI tools to accelerate every homework/project — document token usage and cost
Learn to write effective system prompts for repeated tasks (code review, debugging, test writing)
Build one small automation using an AI API (OpenAI, Anthropic, or Gemini API)
Explore LangChain or LlamaIndex for chaining AI calls together

pillars.title: Build a Portfolio of Real Projects
pillars.description: Your GPA tells employers what you were taught; your portfolio tells them what you can actually do. Where every applicant claims 'strong AI skills,' a deployed project is the proof that stands out.
milestones: Deployed: It must be live at a URL (not just running on your laptop). Use Vercel, Railway, Render, or HuggingFace Spaces — all have free tiers.
Documented: A clear README explaining: the problem, your approach, tech stack, token optimization decisions, and how to run it.
Functional: It actually works. A broken demo is worse than no demo.
Original: Not a tutorial clone. Solve a real problem, even a small one.

Measurable: Includes at least one metric — cost per query, response time, accuracy rate, or number of active users. VIDEO MODULE 3: Build Your First AI Portfolio Project in a Weekend (5 min) > How to pick a problem that is small enough to finish and real enough to impress > The documentation template that gets your README noticed on GitHub > How to add a token usage dashboard so your project shows cost-awareness 2.3 Phase-by-Phase Roadmap
Create a polished GitHub profile: professional photo, bio, pinned repos, README profile
Set up a free personal domain using GitHub Pages or Vercel (yourname.dev
Choose your primary tech stack and commit to it for your first 2 projects
Star and study 5 open-source AI projects in your area — learn how they structure code

pillars.title: Enter the Gig Economy — Starting Week 3
pillars.description: Freelancing is more than extra money — it's proof of real client work, business communication skills, and a track record no internship can replicate. You start in Week 3.
milestones: Create profiles on: Upwork, Fiverr, and Contra (takes about 2-3 hours total) (Account Setup)

Write a compelling bio focused on specific services — be specific, not generic Bad: 'I am a CS student who loves coding.' Good: 'I build Python automation scripts and AI chatbots for small businesses.'
Set your initial rate low to build reviews: $15-25/hr on Upwork, $25-50 per project on Fiverr
Complete your profile 100% — incomplete profiles are filtered out by platform algorithms
On Fiverr: create 3 specific gigs with clear deliverables before applying anywhere
On Upwork: apply to 5 jobs per day in your niche. Write custom proposals — never use templates (First Client)
On Fiverr: create gigs tied to exactly what you are learning that week (e.g., 'I will build a Python web scraper')
On Contra: set up a project portfolio page — it functions as a mini portfolio site
Reach out to 5 local small businesses (restaurant, salon, real estate agent) — offer a small automation or AI chatbot for $50-100

pillars.title: Build a Personal Brand & Network Online
pillars.description: Your personal brand answers 'who are you professionally, and why should I care?' In a sea of identical degrees, a visible online presence is what makes recruiters reach out to you.
milestones: Upgrade your LinkedIn profile completely: professional headshot, headline that says what you do, about section that tells a story
Add all projects, skills, and any work/volunteer experience
Connect with 50 people you already know: professors, classmates, family friends in tech
Follow 20 thought leaders in your target field and engage with their posts (comment thoughtfully) Month 2
Post your first LinkedIn article: 'What I learned building my first AI app' or 'My journey learning [X]' (Content Start)
Commit to posting 1x per week — even short posts (3-5 paragraphs) about what you are working on
Post format that works: Problem you faced → How you solved it → Key lesson learned
Cross-post to dev.to or Hashnode for additional visibility in the developer community
Document your gig profile setup as a post — authenticity outperforms polish Month 2-3

pillars.title: Target the Hidden Job Market via Micro-Internships
pillars.description: The hidden job market is the 70–80% of jobs never publicly posted — filled through referrals, direct outreach, and niche platforms. Micro-internships are short paid sprints that open those doors.
milestones: Sign up for Parker Dewey (the leading micro-internship platform) and complete your profile (Discovery)
Sign up for Forage — complete 1 free virtual experience program from a company you like
Browse Wellfound (formerly AngelList Talent) for early-stage startup roles
Make a list of 20 startups in your area of interest from Crunchbase or Product Hunt Month 1
Complete 2-3 Forage virtual experience programs — these are used by companies to pre-screen candidates (Applications)
Apply to 3-5 Parker Dewey projects per week — write custom applications for each
Identify 5 startups on your list that you would love to work for and research them deeply
Draft a 'cold outreach' message template to founders (see script below) Month 2-3
Send cold outreach LinkedIn messages to 10 startup founders or engineering leads per week (Direct Outreach)

pillars.title: Stack Certifications Strategically
pillars.description: Certifications don't replace skills or projects, but they're a powerful signal of competence when you lack years of experience. The right certs, strategically chosen, get you past the screen.
milestones: Priority 1: AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner (entry level, 1-2 months prep, $100 exam) (Dev Focus)
Priority 2: Meta Front-End or Back-End Developer Certificate (Coursera, 6-8 months, $50/month)
Priority 3: GitHub Actions or Kubernetes basics (free certs, high demand in DevOps roles)
Bonus: Google Project Management Certificate (for dev lead / product roles) Data Science
Priority 1: Google Data Analytics Certificate (Coursera, 6 months, $50/month) (Analytics Focus)
Priority 2: Databricks Certified Data Analyst Associate (free exam voucher often available)
Priority 3: Tableau Desktop Specialist or Microsoft Power BI Data Analyst
Bonus: AWS Certified Machine Learning — Specialty (advanced, but very high-paying) Information Systems
Priority 1: Google Project Management Certificate — signals business + tech bridge (Business + Tech)

pillars.title: Master the Tools Industry Actually Uses
pillars.description: Universities teach programming concepts; industry hires people who know specific tools. This pillar closes the gap between what you learn in class and what you need on day one.
milestones: Go beyond basic 'git add/commit/push' — learn: branching, merging, rebasing, cherry-pick (Git Mastery)
Learn GitFlow (feature branches, develop branch, release branches) — this
Practice submitting pull requests and reviewing others' code on GitHub
Complete the free 'Learn Git Branching' interactive tutorial (learngitbranching.js.org)
Set up a clean, consistent commit message practice (Conventional Commits format)
Master SQL beyond basic SELECT: JOINs, GROUP BY, window functions, CTEs, subqueries (SQL + Data Tools)
Practice on real datasets using Mode Analytics (free), Kaggle, or SQLZoo
Learn at least one BI tool: Power BI (free desktop version) or Tableau Public (free)
Connect SQL to Python using pandas + SQLAlchemy — this combo is used in 80% of data roles

pillars.title: Solve Real Problems in Communities You Care About
pillars.description: The most overlooked career accelerator is also the most fulfilling: using your skills to solve real problems for real people in communities you're already part of.
milestones: Make a list of communities you are part of: cultural student organizations, sports teams, religious groups, local nonprofits, campus departments, (Identify Needs) neighborhood associations
For each community, ask: 'What process is slow, broken, or done manually that I could automate?'
Common opportunities: event scheduling, volunteer coordination, donation tracking, communication, waitlist management, resource directories, tutoring matching
Pick ONE community and ONE specific problem to solve first Month 2-3
Have a 30-minute meeting with the community leader to understand the exact pain point (Build & Deploy)
Build the simplest possible version that actually works — do not over- engineer Example: A Google Form + Apps Script automation might be all they need
Deploy it and get it being actively used by at least 10 people
Measure something: time saved, signups increased, errors reduced — whatever is relevant Month 4-6
Write a case study: the problem, your solution, the outcome with numbers (Document & Expand) Example: 'Reduced volunteer scheduling time from 3 hours to 20 minutes for [Organization]'

B_fast_track.title: 4-Week Fast Track — Prompt Like a Pro

days.deliverable: Read: OpenAI tokenizer docs + tiktoken library Exercise: Count tokens in 5 prompts you wrote before — see the waste
Study: temperature, top_p, max_tokens, stop sequences Exercise: Run same prompt at temp=0 vs temp=1 — document the difference
Study: Context window limits (GPT-4o: 128K, Claude 3.5: 200K, Gemini 1.5: 1M) Learn: What happens when context overflows — silent truncation is dangerous
Study: When to use GPT-4o-mini vs GPT-4o vs o3 vs Claude Haiku vs Sonnet Key insight: Do NOT use the most powerful model for simple tasks
Study: Zero-shot, one-shot, few-shot prompting — when each applies Learn: System prompts vs user prompts vs assistant prefill
Read: 'The Real Carbon Cost of an AI Token' (ditchcarbon.com) Calculate: Estimate the CO2 cost of your last week of AI usage
Build: A Python token counter that analyzes any prompt and reports: token count, estimated cost at GPT-4o rates, estimated CO2 equivalent, and a wasteful pattern flag

weeks.focus: Prompt Like a Pro + Scale Thinking

days.deliverable: Study the SCALE framework above — memorize it Exercise: Take 5 prompts you have written before. Re-write each using SCALE
Apply all 10 optimization techniques from this handbook Exercise: Take a bloated prompt (50+ tokens) and compress it to under 20 tokens without losing output quality
Study: What idempotency means in distributed systems Tolerance (Wikipedia + Martin Fowler blog) Read: 'Designing for failure' — AWS Well-Architected Framework (free online)
Exercise: Prompt for code that processes a 1MB file. Then extend the same prompt for 1TB. Document: What changes in the prompt? What new constraints appear?
Run the same complex coding task on: GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o, Claude Haiku, Claude Sonnet Compare: Output quality, token count, cost, and latency
Review 10 pieces of code you or AI has generated before Identify: Which anti-patterns are present? No error handling? No scale constraints? No idempotency?
Build: A prompt engineering audit tool — Python script that takes a prompt as input and flags: missing scale requirements, missing error handling, missing output format spec, estimated token waste
Apply SCALE framework to design your project — document all 5 questions Draw architecture diagram (Excalidraw — free)
Use SCALE-optimized prompts exclusively — no lazy junior prompts Build core data processing logic with AI assistance
Implement idempotency — checkpoint files or database state tracking Test: Run the pipeline twice on the same input. Verify no duplicate processing.
Add structured logging (Python: structlog or loguru) Logging Implement retry logic with exponential backoff
Run your AI session logs through the token counter you built in Week 1 Document: How many tokens did this project use? At what cost?
Deploy to Railway or Render (free tier) Set up GitHub Actions for automated testing on every commit (CI/CD)
Post on LinkedIn: 'Week 3 of Code For Good fast track. I built [project]. Here is the thing I learned about prompting for production code that no one was teaching me.'
Rewrite your LinkedIn headline: not 'CS Student' but 'AI- Augmented Developer | Enterprise Prompt Engineering | Distributed Systems'
Pick ONE: AWS Cloud Practitioner (exam: $100, 1-2 weeks Track prep) OR Google Data Analytics Certificate (Coursera, 4-6 weeks) OR Databricks Associate (free exam voucher)
Create profiles on Upwork and Contra Your niche: 'I build token-optimized AI pipelines and prompt engineering workflows for businesses'
Study: The 5 prompt engineering questions hiring managers Technical now ask (see box below) Practice: Answer 'How would you optimize this prompt for production?' live without notes
Identify 10 startups building AI products (Product Hunt, Wellfound, Y Combinator list) Send 10 cold LinkedIn messages using the template from the Daily Outreach Plan
Write your first Upwork proposal for a relevant gig Proposal Apply SCALE thinking to the proposal itself — understand their scale, their constraints, their output needs
Record a 5-minute Loom: 'Here is what I built and learned in 4 weeks at Code For Good' Post on LinkedIn: Full reflection — what you can now do that you could not 4 weeks ago
